paper_title: Uniform Diffusion Models Revisited: Leave-One-Out Denoiser and Absorbing State Reformulation

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Discrete diffusion models are often trained through clean-data prediction, but the prediction can be used in different ways to define the reverse dynamics. In Masked Diffusion Models (MDM) these choices largely coincide, whereas in Uniform Diffusion Models (UDM) they do not. We show that the standard plug-in bridge parameterization for UDM is not optimized by the denoising posterior, but by a leave-one-out posterior that predicts each clean token without using its own noisy observation. This identifies a mismatch between the plug-in ELBO and the usual cross-entropy denoising objective. We characterize the leave-one-out target and derive exact conversions between the denoiser, the leave-one-out posterior, and the score. These conversions allow us to disentangle parameterization and training objective. Our results also lead to inference improvements without any additional training through an informed predictor-corrector sampler and improved temperature sampling based on the leave-one-out predictor. We further introduce an absorbing-state reformulation of uniform diffusion that preserves the UDM joint law while decomposing it into masked-diffusion-like sampling operations, with simpler denoising posteriors, carry-over unmasking, and a natural remasking mechanism. On language modeling, leave-one-out parameterizations consistently improve UDM generation, while the absorbing construction matches or surpasses masked diffusion. These results suggest that the empirical gap between masked and uniform diffusion is driven less by the choice of marginals themselves than by parameterization and sampling design. The code and models can be found at https://github.com/samsongourevitch/rev_udm.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Discrete diffusion models have emerged as strong alternative to autoregressive generation for language and other structured discrete domains [9, 14, 20]. Unlike left-to-right models, they support parallel token updates, controllable infilling, and flexible trade-off between compute and sample quality at inference time. Recent models show that they can scale competitively on modern text benchmarks [16, 19, 23, 26]. Although early work emphasized Uniform Diffusion Models (UDM), in which each position is replaced by uniformly sampled token [1, 12, 28], more recent approaches have relied on Masked Diffusion Models (MDM), where corruption replaces tokens by separate mask symbol, motivated by their better perplexity at large vocabulary size [16, 26, 27]. This empirical shift left open fundamental questions regarding parameterization choices in UDMs. To make * Equal contribution Corresponding authors: samson.gourevitch@polytechnique.edu, yazid.janati@mbzuai.ac.ae and dario.shariatian@inria.fr Preprint. this question precise, we distinguish three common parameterizations: the network prediction can be used as (i) score parameterizing rate matrix [16, 24, 32], (ii) denoiser, where the network assigns probabilities to possible clean x0, and the reverse transition is computed as the mixture of the x0-conditioned reverse transitions qs0,t(x0, xt), weighted by these probabilities [1, 10], or (iii) direct clean-token proxy ˆxθ 0(xt, t), plugged instead of x0 in qs0,t(x0, xt) [12, 26, 28, 34]. Contributions. In this paper, we first show that for uniform diffusion the plug-in bridge parameterization, the third option above, does not reach its optimum for the standard ELBO loss at the denoising posterior, which has been the central quantity in diffusion models. We show that for this plug-in parameterization, the ELBO is instead minimized by the leave-one-out posterior: to predict the clean token at position ℓ, the model uses the noisy tokens at all positions except ℓ. This distinction is mostly hidden in masked diffusion because the parameterizations (ii) and (iii) coincide. This observation exposes mismatch between two standard objectives for UDMs: the plug-in ELBO targets leave-one-out posterior, whereas the usual cross-entropy objective targets the plain denoising posterior. We use the conversion formulas between these two quantities to train either parameterization with either objective, namely denoiser through the ELBO or leave-one-out predictor through cross-entropy. Empirically, across objectives and samplers, we find that parameterizing the leave-one-out posterior consistently improves generative performance. These conversions are also useful at inference time. They turn trained denoiser into the leave-one-out quantities needed for an informed predictor-corrector sampler, in the spirit of [38], without training an auxiliary model. They also indicate where to apply sampling heuristics: applying top-p or temperature to the leave-one-out predictor, rather than to the denoiser itself, improves generation at no additional training cost. We finally revisit the gap between uniform and masked diffusion processes [26, 33, 34]. We ask whether this gap is caused by the difference in marginals themselves, or by the parameterization and sampling scheme usually paired with each process. To separate these effects, we propose two absorbing-state constructions of uniform diffusion that preserves the UDM joint law while inheriting structural simplifications of MDM. We coin these Absorbing State Uniform Diffusion Model (AUDM) and Masked Uniform Diffusion Model (MUDM). We show that MUDM makes it posibble to reuse the MDM denoiser and obtain joint process with UDM joint law. As for AUDM, it reaches perplexity and generation quality comparable to masked diffusion. The empirical advantage of masked diffusion therefore appears to stem not from masked versus uniform marginals, but from parameterization choices that can be transferred to model with UDM marginals. We validate our theoretical insights and methods through extensive experiments on large scale language modeling tasks as well as smaller scale Sudoku task. In all the cases considered, the leaveone-out parameterization performs better than its denoiser counterpart, and our absorbing construction is on par or slightly better than masked diffusion models. , and write = {p RK : Notation. sections.title: 4 Bridging Masked and Uniform Diffusion
sections.content: In the previous section we clarified the difference between existing parameterizations of UDMs. We now give an alternative view: uniform diffusion can be represented as an absorbing-state diffusion after conditioning on random sequence of absorbing states. This perspective is useful since it bridges uniform and masked diffusion. This bridge can be made exact and we show in Section 4.3 construction that preserves UDM joint law while using masked diffusion denoiser.

sections.title: 5 Related Works
sections.content: Leave-one-out denoiser. Categorical ratio matching [32] trains discrete diffusion models by learning the concrete score st; by (12), learning the conditional distribution in (15), which is also 8 Figure 1: Comparison between the denoiser and leave-one-out parameterizations. Left: validation perplexity during training. Right: top-p sampling Gen-PPL frontier, obtained by sweeping (p [0.8, 1.0]). Topp is applied to the LOO denoiser and denoiser respectively. The curves are shown only up to entropy 5.6. The vertical line corresponds to the dataset entropy leave-one-out, is enough to recover the score and has motivated the use of the Hollow Transformer architecture. As recalled in Appendix A.4, using the Hollow Transformer is essential for their loss function to recover the correct conditional distribution; otherwise, the model can simply learn the identity function. Here we show instead that standard UDMs trained with the bridge parameterization and ELBO already target the same leave-one-out object, and we propose simple cross-entropy objective whose unique minimizer is the leave-one-out conditional distribution, without imposing architectural constraints. Predictor-corrector samplers. Predictor-corrector samplers alternate reverse transition step with corrector kernel that preserves the marginal pt. For discrete diffusion, [2] propose an uninformed birth-death corrector whose rates combine the forward and reverse generators, and ReMDM [35] introduces remasking in masked diffusion, which can be read as predictor steps interleaved with single corrector step. Closer to our setting, [38] train an auxiliary leave-one-out model to build an informed Gibbs corrector. For UDMs, our key observation is that, leveraging the leaveone-out representation, this correction does not require separately trained model. Finally, in the recent work [7] the authors propose the Ψ-samplers framework, which recovers specific instances of known predictor-corrector samplers. Interestingly, when specialized to UDMs, their sampler assumes that the trained network has learned LOO denoiser and thus does not work when the network has learned denoiser instead. We provide in Appendix E.1 the general form that applies in both cases. UDMs with augmented states. Several recent works also augment the state space of UDMs, but for different purposes. [40] introduces routing variables that decide, in confidence-dependent way, which positions should be treated as already clean during sampling. [15] conditions on binary corruption mask and learns separate planner that chooses which positions to denoise as well as [5] instead uses latent mask-conditioned denoiser, leading to planner-denoiser factorization. transition times, which reveals an exact connection between UDMs and masked denoising, although the implemented sampler ultimately plugs in an ordinary denoiser that is not conditioned on these times. Our absorbing-state construction is different in spirit: the auxiliary variable is part of an exact lifted model, and the resampling step is chosen so that marginalizing the augmentation recovers the UDM reverse-chain law rather than only providing decoding heuristic. We give the detailed comparison in Appendix B.3.

We evaluate primarily on language modeling and use Sudoku as smaller structured discrete check. All the experimental details are provided in Appendix I. Language modeling. We experiment on One Billion Word (LM1B [4]) and OpenWebText (OWT [8]), following [16, 26, 27]. LM1B uses the bert-base-uncased tokenizer with = 128, while OWT uses the gpt2 tokenizer with = 1024. All models use the same DiT-based architecture as [16, 27] and are trained from scratch for 1M steps with batch size 512. To assess sample quality, we report GPT-2 Large generative perplexity (Gen-PPL) with the OWT-trained model. Similarly to [15, 25] we rely on the generative frontier where we plot the Gen-PPL as function of 1-gram entropy. We vary the entropy using either temperature or nucleus sampling. This evaluation is substantially more informative than reporting the performance at single entropy, as emphasized by [25] since different entropies may reverse the Gen-PPL ordering of two models. Sudoku. We also run 9 9 Sudoku completion experiment to test the same modeling choices on different structured discrete task. We use similar setup to [36]. The model is smaller DiT with about 6M parameters, trained on 50k-puzzle Sudoku split. We sweep the learning rate independently for every method family and report, for each method, the learning rate that maximizes the average validation solve rate across seeds and NFEs. The full setup is summarized in Table 5.

sections.title: 6.1 Leave-one-out parameterization
sections.content: LOO denoiser vs denoiser. We first isolate the effect of the parameterization by training UDMs with either the denoiser or the LOO denoiser parameterization under matched architectures. We consider both cross-entropy and continuous-time ELBO training for both parameterizations, using the standard UDM ELBO of [28] and the Maximal Coupling2 ELBO derived in Proposition 11. The configuration ELBO with the denoiser parameterization is the continuous-time analogue of D3PM Uniform trained without the auxiliary cross-entropy term in the hybrid objective [1, Equation 5]. Although [28] compare against D3PM Uniform, their comparison also changes the training objective: the D3PM baseline is trained with discrete-time loss, while the plug-in UDM model uses continuous-time loss, which already improves perplexity; see [28, Figure 2]. We therefore train both the denoiser and LOO parameterizations with the same continuous-time loss, isolating the effect of the parameterization itself. The validation curves on LM1B and OWT, on the left plot of Figure 1 show that the LOO parameterization improves optimization, with better perplexity throughout the training. The zero-shot results in Table 1 show that this trend transfers beyond OWT where in the three settings the LOO improves over its denoiser counterpart on almost all the datasets considered. We then evaluate generation with top-p frontiers, sweeping [0.8, 1.0] and plotting Gen-PPL against 1-gram entropy. The right panel of Figure 1 shows that the LOO parameterization improves the frontier in the relevant low-entropy regime. 2This model is related to the DDIM sampler for UDMs derived in [30, Appendix A] and is used to confirm that our findings carry over to different processes. This process is discussed extensively in Appendix G.3 10 Cross-entropy training. Figure 1 shows that cross-entropy training with the denoiser is the weakest combination, despite its simplicity. Using the LOO parameterization largely removes this drawback: cross-entropy becomes competitive with ELBO training while remaining simpler to implement. Our practical takeaway is therefore to use the LOO denoiser as the default UDM prediction target, especially with cross-entropy training. Applying top-p to the LOO denoiser. In the comparison in Figure 1, top-p is applied to the learned primitive itself: to the LOO denoiser before using Bridge Plug-in and to the denoiser before using Marginalization. useful consequence of our conversion formulas is that one can also start from denoiser-trained model, convert the denoiser to the LOO representation, apply top-p there, and then sample with the bridge plug-in parameterization. This gives non-negligible improvement of the frontier at no additional cost when using top-p sampling and reduces the gap with the LOO parameterization, as shown in Figure 2. Figure 2: Top-p sampling Gen-PPL frontier, obtained by sweeping (p [0.8, 1.0]). Top-p sampling is applied to the denoiser, denoiser converted into LOO denoiser and LOO denoiser. As detailed in Appendix E, the LOO parameterization gives access to corrector step without training an auxiliary model, in contrast to [38]. We use the confidencebased resampling rule of [38], with 1, 15 (cid:75) parallel token updates per corrector step. We show the results for trained LOO denoiser on the left panel of Figure 3 and for trained denoiser converted into LOO denoiser on the right panel. The resulting predictor-corrector frontiers Pareto-dominate the ancestral temperature frontiers in both cases. In addition to being substantially better, key advantage is that this correction is cheap: in our implementation, the predictor-corrector sampler has the same runtime as the regular ancestral sampler. For completeness, we also report the comparison with top-p sampling in Figure 9. sections.title: 6.2 Absorbing-state uniform diffusion
sections.content: We finally evaluate the absorbing-state uniform diffusion model of Section 4. AUDM uses the same 170M-parameter DiT backbone as the UDM and MDM baselines, and adds 7M-parameter module to process the auxiliary noise variable , corresponding to 4.1% parameter overhead. AUDM gives the best likelihood results among the uniform-diffusion variants in Table 1. It improves over UDM on the OWT validation distribution and on every zero-shot dataset. It also outperforms MDM on all zero-shot datasets, although MDM remains slightly better on OWT validation perplexity. At sampling time, Figure 4 shows that AUDM remains competitive along the generative frontier, and is often slightly better than MDM, uniformly across NFEs. Thus, the absorbing-state construction preserves the marginals of UDMs while improving its likelihood. 11 Figure 3: Gen-PPL frontiers. Temperature sampling Gen-PPL frontier, obtained by sweeping the temperature in [0.8, 1.10]. Left: predictor-corrector sampling using trained LOO denoiser against ancestral sampling using the same LOO denoiser. Right: predictor-corrector sampling using trained plain denoiser converted at inference time to LOO against ancestral sampling using the same plain denoiser. The vertical line corresponds to the dataset entropy Figure 4: Left: Sudoku solve rate as function of NFE for the best learning rate of each method. Right: AUDM, resampled AUDM, UDM, and MDM Gen-PPL frontier. The vertical line corresponds to the dataset entropy. The Sudoku results in Figure 4 give smaller-scale check of the same conclusions outside language modeling. The best solve rates are obtained by UDMs trained with the leave-one-out target, with cross-entropy and ELBO both reaching nearly perfect accuracy. UDM also outperforms MDM on this task. We see that AUDM improves over MDM even though they share structural properties, and interestingly, the ReAUDM construction improves over AUDM even though they use the same checkpoint and the sampling scheme does not add any additional cost or learned component.

sections.title: 7 Conclusion
sections.content: We revisited uniform discrete diffusion from two complementary perspectives: the parameterization of the reverse kernel and the design of the forward process. On the parameterization side, we showed that the bridge plug-in objective of UDMs is optimized not by the standard denoising posterior but by leave-one-out posterior. We derived exact conversions between the denoiser, the leave-one-out predictor, and the score, which separate the choice of training target from the choice of sampling parameterization. In practice, this viewpoint leads to simple cross-entropy objective for the leaveone-out predictor that improves over the standard cross-entropy parameterization, way to apply top-p or temperature in the most effective representation, and predictor-corrector sampler that can be obtained from trained model without any auxiliary network. On the process-design side, we introduced an absorbing-state reformulation of uniform diffusion, together with resampled version that recovers the full UDM reverse chain. By conditioning on the transition time, we showed that it is even possible to re-use the masked diffusion denoiser while keeping UDM joint law. These constructions show that masked-diffusion-like denoisers, carry-over structure, and remasking can be transferred to model that remains faithful to the uniform-diffusion viewpoint. Empirically, leave-one-out parameterizations consistently improve optimization and the generative frontier of UDMs, while AUDM and ReAUDM match or surpass masked diffusion on 12 several frontier evaluations while improving likelihood within the uniform-diffusion family. Altogether, our results indicate that the practical gap between masked and uniform diffusion is driven less by the corruption marginals themselves than by the learned representation and the inference scheme built on top of them. Limitations and future work. Our evidence for the advantage of the leave-one-out target remains mainly empirical, and theoretical explanation for its improved generative frontier remains open. In addition, although AUDM and ReAUDM clarify how masked-style structure can be transferred to uniform diffusion and substantially improve likelihood, the best generative frontiers are still obtained by leave-one-out UDMs. Improving these absorbing-state models through better parameterizations or sampling schemes is therefore an important direction for future work. Finally, when training the AUDM denoiser, we condition on one-hot absorbing tokens. natural extension would be to replace this with simplex-valued conditioning vector, possibly with mass on the mask, so as to interpolate within single network between AUDM-type posteriors, the standard UDM denoiser obtained at 1/K, and more general hybrid corruption mechanisms. Acknowledgments. providing helpful comments.

sections.title: Appendix Outline
sections.content: The appendix is organized as follows. Appendix proves the leave-one-out optimality result, gives the conversion formulas between the leave-one-out denoiser, denoiser and score, and derives the Gibbs conditional used by the predictor-corrector sampler. Appendix gives the full derivation of AUDM and ReAUDM, including the continuous-time AUDM objective and the lifted resampling construction. Appendix gives the masked-diffusion lifting of UDM and proves Proposition 4. Appendix discusses the bridge extension underlying the plug-in parameterization. Appendix details the predictor-corrector sampler. In Appendix we provide the continuous-time CTMC perspective of discrete diffusion models. In Appendix we detail Maximal Coupling Uniform Diffusion and its ELBO. Appendix discusses the leave-one-out invariance diagnostic. Appendix gives experimental details and additional results. A.6 An example of leave-one-out and denoiser differences In order to confirm some intuition on the difference between the leave-one-out and the denoiser, we train uniform diffusion model with the Bridge Plug-in parameterization that reaches leave-oneout denoiser at optimality and model with the Marginalization parameterization that recovers denoiser. We then plot heatmap corresponding to the probabilities output by the leave-one-out and the denoiser before passing them into their respective updates during sampling. We show the results in Figure 5. In this case, the difference is mainly on the edges of the digits, where the denoiser is more confident than the leave-one-out posterior. This yields Gibbs sampler with invariant distribution pt. Predictor-corrector samplers. Predictor-corrector schemes were introduced in the continuous setting in [31]. They alternate predictor step, which moves along the reverse dynamics, and corrector step, which applies an MCMC kernel preserving the current marginal. They make use of the fact that during training, we learn the approximate score sθ(t, xt) log pt(xt) to construct Langevin dynamic dXt = sθ(t, xt)dt + In the discrete setting, [2] proposed an analogue based on the reverse CTMC for the predictor and on forward-backward CTMC for the corrector. The role of the corrector is to improve the sample quality at fixed time before continuing the reverse trajectory. The identity (36) provides another such corrector: instead of using an uninformed transition that preserves pt, one may apply Gibbs updates based on the exact one-coordinate conditionals of pt. 2dWt which approximately leaves pt invariant. Informed Gibbs corrector. This observation is particularly useful in UDM. Indeed, Proposition 1 shows that, under the Bridge Plug-in parameterization, the network already learns the leave-one-out object needed to evaluate the Gibbs conditional. Therefore, unlike [38], no auxiliary leave-oneout model is required. Among exact Gibbs schemes, deterministic scan and random scan are the canonical choices. In practice, however, it is often more efficient to concentrate the corrector effort on the positions where the predictor is least confident. Following [38], we therefore use the margin score cmargin ℓ (xt) := log pℓ t(xℓ txℓ ) max x=xℓ log pℓ t(xxℓ ), 34 which measures the gap between the current token and the most likely alternative. Low values of cmargin (xt) correspond to unlikely positions, and the corrector updates the coordinates with the ℓ smallest margins first. This is the practical scheme used in our experiments. As in [38], one may also update several coordinates in parallel, in the spirit of Hogwild Gibbs sampling [13]. This parallel version should be viewed as heuristic acceleration of the exact single-coordinate Gibbs corrector. i=1, number of parallel updates k, and model providing pθ siti and ˆxloo 0si () Algorithm 5 Predictor-corrector sampler with margin-based Gibbs corrector Require: time grid 0 = t0 < t1 < < tn = 1 Require: integers (mi)n Ensure: sample approximately distributed according to p0 1: Draw xtn p1 2: for = n, 1, . . . , 1 do 3: 4: 5: 6: Draw predictor state xsi pθ for = 1, . . . , mi do for ℓ = 1, . . . , do (xℓ si ) qℓ (xti) siti Define pℓ si Compute cℓ log pℓ si si0(ˆxloo 0si xℓ (xℓ si si (xsi)ℓ) ) maxx=xℓ si log pℓ si (xxℓ si ) end for Let ℓr,1, . . . , ℓr,k be the coordinates with smallest scores cℓ for = 1, . . . , do Resample xℓr,j ) in parallel si (xℓr,j si pℓr,j si 7: 8: 9: 10: end for end for Set xti1 := xsi 11: 12: 13: 14: 15: end for 16: return x0 in parallel over ℓ Figure 3 shows that the Gibbs corrector significantly improves sample quality at comparable computational cost. As already emphasized in [2], predictor-corrector schemes are often particularly effective in the discrete setting. The reason is that corrector step may modify the value of token altogether, rather than merely perturbing the state locally. As consequence, correcting few uncertain positions can substantially alter the subsequent reverse trajectory. This effect is typically stronger than in continuous domains, where corrector steps usually act as local refinements of an already smooth state. In discrete domains, by contrast, the uncertainty is concentrated on finite set of alternatives, and resolving it can produce qualitatively different samples. This explains why corrector-based methods have become central tool in discrete diffusion models; see for instance [2, 35, 38]. Leave-one-out and Hollow Transformers As noted in Section 3.2, the ℓ-th component of the leave-one-out posterior is independent of the local observation xℓ t. This is necessary structural property of the optimum identified in Proposition 1. It therefore has two practical consequences. First, it provides direct diagnostic for suboptimality: after converting trained model to the leave-one-out representation, any residual dependence of the ℓ-th output on xℓ certifies that the model has not reached the leave-one-out optimum. Second, it suggests an architectural design: instead of hoping that optimization learns this invariance, one may try to enforce it by construction. LOO sensitivity diagnostic. We check the sensitivity of the leave-one-out posterior to the local on some of the models that we have trained. More precisely, we vary xℓ observation xℓ while keeping xℓ fixed and measure the induced change in the ℓ-th leave-one-out prediction. The results are reported in Figure 6. On low-dimensional examples such as MNIST, the leave-one-out posterior is indeed nearly insensitive to the local observation. Moreover, the denoiser converted into the leave-one-out representation and the leave-one-out predictor trained directly produce almost identical sensitivity curves. This strongly suggests that both models are already close to their respective optima, in which case the conversion formula of Proposition 5 is effectively exact. 40 (a) MNIST, converted denoiser (b) MNIST, direct leave-one-out (c) OWT, converted denoiser (d) OWT, direct leave-one-out Figure 6: Sensitivity of the leave-one-out prediction to the local observation xℓ t. On MNIST, the converted denoiser and the directly trained leave-one-out model are both nearly insensitive to xℓ t. On OWT, the sensitivity remains visible for both models, but it is lower for the directly trained leaveone-out predictor than for the converted denoiser. This indicates that the leave-one-out invariance is not automatically recovered in the high-dimensional regime, and that proximity to this invariance is informative about the quality of the attained optimum. On larger sequence models, such as the transformer trained on OWT, the sensitivity remains clearly nonzero. This shows that in realistic high-dimensional settings the leave-one-out invariance is not automatically recovered by finite-capacity models trained with standard objectives. More generally, the sensitivity diagnostic appears to be well aligned with downstream performance: models that are closer to the leave-one-out optimum (using the local sensitivity as proxy) also tend to be the ones that perform better generatively. This observation naturally suggests enforcing the invariance directly in the architecture. For transformers, however, doing so while preserving access to the rest of the context is not entirely straightforward. Hollow transformer. proposal for such an architecture is the hollow transformer introduced by [32] and later used by [38]. The idea is to combine two autoregressive streams, one left-to-right and one right-to-left, and to offset the representations so that the output at position ℓ never attends to the input token at the same position, while still depending on all the other positions. Equivalently, in the continuous relaxation used to describe the architecture, the ℓ-th output is constrained to satisfy (xt, t)ℓ/xℓ = 0. This makes the architecture structurally compatible with the leave-one-out target. In [38], this construction is used in the absorbing setting to learn the additional leave-one-out information needed by the informed corrector without modifying the ELBO objective. The same motivation applies here: if the target at position ℓ should ignore xℓ t, it is appealing to remove that dependence by design rather than by optimization. This inductive bias, however, is not free. Besides requiring two autoregressive streams, the hollow transformer also restricts the information flow relative to standard transformer, since the output at position ℓ cannot aggregate the full context in single attention step. More precisely, when computing the ℓ-th output, the tokens to the left and to the right of ℓ interact within their respective 41 Table 2: Training setup for the language-model experiments. This weakens the directness of the attention mechanism and makes the prediction problem harder. In our own experiments on LM1B, this translated into worse optimization and worse final performance than the corresponding standard DiT models, in line with the observations of [38]. We therefore do not use the hollow transformer in the main experiments. Our view is that it remains conceptually clean way to enforce the leave-one-out structure, but in large-scale language modeling this structural benefit can be outweighed by the optimization cost.

sections.title: I Experimental Details
sections.content: I.1 Setup and hyperparameters We report here the details relevant to the language-model experiments. We use the same setting as [16, 26]. All models are trained with the same DDiT backbone and the same optimization scheme, and the only dataset-specific architectural change is the sequence length. Both LM1B and OWT are trained from scratch for 106 steps with global batch size 512, Adam with learning rate 3 104, β1 = 0.9, β2 = 0.999, ϵ = 108, no weight decay, gradient clipping at 1.0, and constant learningrate schedule with 2500 warmup steps. Training uses bfloat16, exponential moving average with decay 0.9999, antithetic time sampling, and sampling floor ϵ = 103. Unless stated otherwise, all experiments use seed 1. For generative evaluation we report GPT-2 Large generative perplexity. The sampling uses higher precision float64. These hyperparameters are summarized in Table 2. LM1B is trained and evaluated on the same dataset family with the BERT tokenizer, with sequence length = 128. OWT is trained on the OpenWebText train split and validated on the held-out OpenWebText validation split with the GPT-2 tokenizer, with sequence length = 1024. The zero-shot perplexity evaluation of Table 1 uses the OWT checkpoints and evaluates them on OWT 42 Table 4: Modeling choices for considered methods. In the absorbing-uniform variants, the noised data and the uniform noise variable (xt, ut) are first embedded with the same embedding table with hidden size d, concatenated together and with 1xt=ut, forming vector of dimension 2d + 1, and then passed through an MLP with hidden dimension 4d and output dimension d. This adds 7M parameters, around 4% of the total parameter count. Across all comparisons, we keep the previous training setup fixed and vary only the modeling the training loss, the prediction target and the associated choices that are central to the paper: reverse-process parameterization. The denoiser-based UDM baseline uses the standard denoiser prediction, while the leave-one-out model replaces this with the leave-one-out target. The maximalcoupling models change only the path coupling. The masked baseline uses the standard absorbingstate parameterization of [26] with carry-over unmasking and zero-masking probability. The full top-p frontiers across all NFEs are reported in Figure 8. They confirm the same trend as in the main text: for uniform diffusion, the leave-one-out parameterization is consistently preferable in the low-entropy regime. For the maximal-coupling variants the difference between the two parameterizations is smaller but shows similar trend. Applying nucleus sampling after converting denoiser prediction to the leave-one-out representation also improves the frontier, as shown in Figure 2. The converted denoiser closes substantial part of the gap to the model trained directly with the leave-one-out target, while remaining clearly better than applying top-p to the denoiser itself. The predictor-corrector comparison with filtered top-p sampling is shown in Figure 9. At 128 and 256 NFEs, the two approaches are broadly competitive, while at 512 and 1024 NFEs several predictor-corrector configurations move to strictly better region of the frontier. Performance comparison with AUDM Finally, Figure 10 reports the full frontier comparison for AUDM, resampled AUDM, UDM, and MDM. The two AUDM variants track each other closely and remain competitive throughout the NFE range. They outperform MDM and the denoiser parameterization variants, which is consistent with the main-text conclusion that the absorbing-uniform construction preserves strong sampling behavior while improving likelihood. 43 Figure 7: Generative frontiers with varying temperature across NFEs for the denoiser and leave-oneout parameterizations.
